worth_it_explanation: PMP and PG deals carry a publisher's negotiated rates, and those rates only hold if the deals are active, spending, and priced so they do not collide with the rest of the demand on the same inventory. A deal that has stalled, or a deal floor set below other demand, costs a publisher the premium the deal was meant to protect, with no signal to show it. The check reads the deal setup and recent delivery a publisher already has in SpringServe, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete deal or floor decision the publisher can act on.
intro: SpringServe routes Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) demand alongside the open auction and direct-sold inventory, so a deal's value depends on whether it delivers and on how its price sits next to everything else competing for the same impression. This subcategory covers the health of those deals, whether the deal IDs are active and spending rather than stalled, and the alignment of deal floors and priorities so a deal neither undercuts the rest of the demand nor gets undercut by it. These checks confirm that the deals a publisher negotiated are actually carrying the value they were set up to carry.

audit_checks.title: Deal health is not monitored, so stalled or non-spending deals go unnoticed
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A negotiated PMP or PG deal carries a rate a publisher agreed with a buyer, and that rate only earns revenue while the deal is active and spending. A deal ID that has gone inactive, or one that delivers far below its committed volume, returns no signal unless someone reads the delivery, so the lost revenue stays invisible until a review surfaces it.

educate.base:
A deal in SpringServe represents a negotiated arrangement between a publisher and a buyer that carries its own deal ID, its own price terms, and, for a Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal, a committed volume the buyer agreed to purchase. A Private Marketplace (PMP) deal opens premium video or Connected TV (CTV) inventory to an invited buyer at a negotiated floor, while a PG deal fixes both the price and the number of impressions. In both cases the value to the publisher depends on the deal actually delivering, so the deal ID matters as a thing to watch rather than a thing to set once and forget.

Deals stall for ordinary reasons. A buyer pauses or ends a campaign on their side, a targeting mismatch leaves the deal with no eligible impressions, a creative fails to meet the video or CTV ad spec, or a deal ID is configured but never activated by the buyer. The deal ID still exists in the setup, so a glance at the deal list shows it present, yet the delivery against it has gone to zero or fallen well below what the deal was meant to carry. Nothing in the system raises a hand about this on its own.

For a Programmatic Guaranteed deal the gap has a second cost. The auction type for a PG deal is fixed price, set to 3 in OpenRTB terms, and the deal terms name a fixed number of impressions the buyer committed to buying. A PG deal pacing behind that commitment means the inventory reserved for it is either going unsold or clearing through other demand at a different price, and the buyer's pacing signal, which OpenRTB carries as the must-bid indicator, is the cue that delivery is off track.

A few signs point to deal health going unmonitored:
  • No regular read of revenue, impressions, and spend broken out by deal ID exists
  • Deal IDs sit in the setup with zero or near-zero recent delivery and no record of whether that is expected
  • A Programmatic Guaranteed deal is pacing well behind its committed volume with no one tracking the shortfall
  • A buyer relationship is treated as live because the deal ID was created, without a check that the deal is spending
You may want to confirm that each active deal ID is delivering as expected and that stalled deals are identified rather than assumed healthy. One approach is to read deal-level delivery on a regular cadence and flag any deal whose spend has dropped to zero or fallen below its committed pace for follow-up with the buyer.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster property, premium PMP and PG deals often sit against tentpole content or a launch window, where the value is concentrated in a short period. You may want to watch deal-level delivery closely around those windows. A stalled deal during a tentpole leaves high-value CTV inventory clearing at open-auction prices instead of the negotiated rate.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content video property, deals may be tied to a retail buyer or a seasonal promotion with a defined run. You may want to track delivery against those deals through the promotion. A deal that stalls mid-promotion leaves shoppable video inventory unsold or filled by lower-value demand while the negotiated rate goes uncollected.

learn_more.source_title: Real-time Bidding differences for Programmatic Guaranteed Deals

educate.learn_more_note: SpringServe's detailed configuration documentation for deal setup and deal-level reporting is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the active deal IDs in SpringServe against their recent delivery to see which deals are spending as expected and which have stalled or fallen behind their committed pace.

springserve_ui.label: SpringServe UI
steps: Open the demand area and list the active deals, noting each deal ID, its type (PMP or Programmatic Guaranteed), the buyer, and its price terms.
For each Programmatic Guaranteed deal, record the committed impression volume and the run dates so you have the expected pace to compare against.
Note any deal ID that is present in the setup but shows no recent activity, which points to a deal that may be stalled or never activated by the buyer.

springserve_reporting.label: SpringServe reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by deal ID with revenue, impressions, and spend over a recent window.
Compare each deal's recent delivery against its expected level, and for Programmatic Guaranteed deals compare actual impressions against the committed volume and the dates to gauge pacing.
Record any deal whose spend has dropped to zero, or a PG deal pacing well behind its commitment, so it can be raised with the buyer.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per deal ID, the deal type, the price terms, the expected delivery (and committed volume for Programmatic Guaranteed deals), and the recent actual delivery. A setup where active deals are delivering as expected and stalled deals have been identified and followed up passes. A setup where deal IDs sit with zero or below-pace delivery and no monitoring exists to catch them is a fail recorded with the affected deal IDs and the size of the shortfall.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each deal ID with its expected and actual delivery above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not monitor deal health in SpringServe, so stalled or non-spending Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals can go unnoticed. A deal ID carries a negotiated rate that only earns revenue while the deal is active and spending, and a deal that has paused or fallen behind its committed pace returns no signal on its own, so the lost revenue stays invisible until a review surfaces it. {{voice_recommend}} reading deal-level revenue, impressions, and spend on a regular cadence, comparing each Programmatic Guaranteed deal against its committed volume, and flagging any deal that drops to zero or paces below plan for follow-up with the buyer. After the change, keep the deal-level read on a recurring schedule so a stalled deal is caught while it can still be recovered.

audit_checks.title: Deal floors and priorities are misaligned with the rest of the demand
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal's floor and its priority decide how it competes against the open auction and against other deals on the same impression. A deal floor set below other demand lets that demand win impressions the deal was meant to hold, and a priority that ranks a low-value deal above a higher-value one routes impressions away from the better price, so a misalignment trades revenue on every impression the deal touches.

educate.base:
Every Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal in SpringServe competes for the same video and Connected TV (CTV) impressions as the open auction and any other deals on that inventory, and two settings decide how that competition resolves. The deal floor is the price the deal must clear, which for a PMP deal is the negotiated floor and for a PG deal is the fixed price both sides agreed. The priority is where the deal sits in the order SpringServe considers demand for an impression. A deal earns its negotiated value only when these two settings place it correctly against everything else.

A misaligned floor breaks the arrangement in one of two directions. A deal floor set too low relative to the open auction lets open-auction demand outbid or sit beside the deal at a price the publisher would have preferred to reserve for the deal, so the buyer who negotiated access gets less of the inventory than intended. A deal floor set too high relative to where comparable demand clears can leave the deal with no eligible impressions, so it stalls for a pricing reason rather than a delivery one. The floor has to fit both the negotiated terms and the live clearing prices on that inventory.

Priority cuts the other way. A Programmatic Guaranteed deal usually carries a commitment, so it is meant to win the impressions it targets ahead of non-guaranteed demand, and a priority that ranks it below the open auction risks the publisher failing to deliver the guaranteed volume. A Private Marketplace deal is non-guaranteed, so ranking a low-value PMP deal above a higher-value direct-sold line or a stronger deal routes impressions to the lower price. The order has to reflect both the commitment a deal carries and the value it returns.

A few signs point to floors and priorities being misaligned:
  • A deal floor sits below the average clearing price of the open auction on the same inventory, so open-auction demand competes on impressions the deal was meant to hold
  • A Programmatic Guaranteed deal is ranked below non-guaranteed demand, putting its committed volume at risk
  • A lower-value deal is prioritized ahead of a higher-value deal or direct-sold line on the same inventory
  • Deal floors are set once at deal launch and never reconciled against how clearing prices on that inventory have moved since
You may want to confirm that each deal's floor and priority place it correctly against the open auction and the other demand it competes with. One approach is to compare each deal floor against recent clearing prices on the same inventory, and to check that priority follows both the commitment each deal carries and the value it returns.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster property, premium CTV inventory often clears high in the open auction, so a deal floor set below that clearing price lets the open auction compete on impressions a PMP buyer was invited to. You may want to reconcile deal floors against the live CTV clearing prices and confirm that a guaranteed deal on tentpole inventory ranks ahead of non-guaranteed demand so its committed volume is protected.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content video property, shoppable or high-intent placements can carry strong demand across both deals and the open auction. You may want to check that a retail buyer's deal floor sits at or above where comparable demand clears on that inventory, and that the deal's priority does not rank a lower-value promotion ahead of a higher-value direct-sold placement on the same impressions.

learn_more.source_title: Real-time Bidding differences for Programmatic Guaranteed Deals

educate.learn_more_note: SpringServe's detailed configuration documentation for deal floors and demand priority is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each deal's floor and priority against the open auction and the other demand on the same inventory to see whether the deal is placed to hold the value it was negotiated for.

springserve_ui.label: SpringServe UI
steps: Open the demand area and, for each active deal, record its floor (or fixed price for a Programmatic Guaranteed deal) and its priority relative to the open auction and other demand.
Note for each deal whether it is guaranteed (PG) or non-guaranteed (PMP), which determines where its priority should sit.
Record any case where a deal floor or priority looks out of order against the demand it shares inventory with.

springserve_reporting.label: SpringServe reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the inventory each deal targets, with the open-auction average clearing price and the deal's own clearing price on that inventory.
Compare each deal floor against the open-auction clearing price on the same inventory to see whether the floor sits below, at, or above where other demand clears.
Note any deal whose floor sits below the open auction it competes with, or any Programmatic Guaranteed deal whose delivery suggests it is losing impressions to demand it should rank ahead of.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per deal, the deal floor or fixed price, the priority, the deal type, and the open-auction clearing price on the same inventory. A setup where deal floors sit appropriately against comparable clearing prices and priorities follow each deal's commitment and value passes. A setup where a deal floor sits below the open auction it competes with, or a guaranteed deal ranks below non-guaranteed demand, or a lower-value deal outranks a higher-value one, is a fail recorded with the affected deals and the misaligned setting.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each deal's floor, priority, and the open-auction clearing price on its inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has deal floors and priorities in SpringServe that are misaligned with the rest of the demand on the same inventory. A deal floor set below the open auction lets that demand compete on impressions the deal was meant to hold, and a priority that ranks a low-value deal above a higher-value one routes impressions away from the better price, so the misalignment trades revenue on every impression the deal touches. {{voice_recommend}} comparing each deal floor against recent open-auction clearing prices on the same inventory, confirming that guaranteed deals rank ahead of non-guaranteed demand to protect their committed volume, and ordering priority so it follows both the commitment each deal carries and the value it returns. After the change, recheck deal delivery and clearing prices to confirm each deal holds the value it was negotiated for.
